skill.name: building-chat-interfaces
skill.description: Guides teams through building ChatKit-based AI chat interfaces with custom backends, authentication, context injection, persistence, and Next.js proxy patterns.
skill.summary: Build ChatKit chat interfaces with backend auth, context, persistence, and proxy patterns.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Most static detections are false positives caused by Markdown fences, inline backticks, and illustrative network or environment examples. A real high-risk issue remains because the skill recommends placing access tokens into model instructions for MCP tool calls, which can expose credentials through prompts, logs, or tool traces.

high_findings.title: Access Tokens Embedded in Model Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill advises formatting an access token into a system prompt and instructing the model to include it with MCP tool calls. This can expose credentials through prompt history, model traces, logs, or unintended tool arguments.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section explicitly names an access token and places it inside SYSTEM_PROMPT text. The recommendation is semantically insecure even though it appears educational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Client-Controlled Identity and Page Context Forwarding
medium_findings.description: The examples add user identifiers and page context from browser state into request metadata and headers. This is useful for chat context, but backends must not trust these values without validating the authenticated token.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly forward user-controlled metadata and headers. The risk depends on backend validation, so the finding is high confidence as a design concern but not proof of exploitability.

medium_findings.title: Page Content Sent to Chat Backend
medium_findings.description: The context extraction examples collect URL, title, headings, description, and selected text for chat requests. This can disclose sensitive page data if used on private or regulated content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data collection pattern is explicit and intentional. The severity is context dependent because some applications expect this behavior and can obtain consent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Reported as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections come from Markdown code fences and inline code examples, not executable Ruby backtick operators. No evidence found of a runtime shell command path in the audited skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced documentation snippets or inline identifiers. They are not executable files and do not invoke a shell during skill use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto and Credential File Patterns
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and credential file alerts match ordinary documentation text such as RS256 JWT verification, cookies, and prompt wording. No evidence found of browser credential file access or Windows SAM access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are explanatory code snippets and troubleshooting rows. The flagged words do not indicate file reads, credential scraping, or weak hash use.

low_findings.title: Expected Environment and Network Examples
low_findings.description: The environment variable and fetch detections are expected for backend configuration, JWT verification, CDN loading, and API proxy examples. These patterns need normal deployment review, not a block by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples use environment values and fetch calls for documented application behavior. No evidence found that they exfiltrate secrets to an unrelated endpoint.

dangerous_patterns.title: Token-in-Prompt Authentication Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Embedding access tokens in model instructions is dangerous because prompts may be stored, inspected, or repeated into tool calls. Prefer server-side token exchange or scoped backend mediation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example directly formats access_token into SYSTEM_PROMPT. This is a concrete unsafe authentication pattern, not a scanner artifact.

dangerous_patterns.title: Trust Boundary Blurring in Request Metadata
dangerous_patterns.description: Browser-provided user identity and page metadata are forwarded with chat requests. Treat these values as untrusted unless verified against server-side authentication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The forwarding behavior is visible in the examples. Actual exploitability depends on downstream authorization checks.

content.value_statement: Production chat interfaces need authentication, persistence, streaming, and context without fragile glue code. This skill provides practical ChatKit, React, and Next.js patterns for those integrations.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to extend ChatKitServer with a custom agent response flow.
Shows React useChatKit configuration with custom fetch handling.
Documents Next.js httpOnly cookie proxy patterns for chat requests.
Covers database persistence setup and connection pool warmup.
Describes page context and selected text injection for chat workflows.
Lists common integration pitfalls and fixes for ChatKit deployments.
limitations: It is guidance documentation, not a complete runnable application.
It assumes ChatKit, React, Next.js, and Python backend familiarity.
Some authentication guidance needs security review before production use.
It does not replace official API documentation for version-specific behavior.

use_cases.title: Add authenticated chat to a product UI
use_cases.description: Use the React and proxy patterns to connect a logged-in user interface to a ChatKit backend.

use_cases.title: Connect ChatKit to a custom agent backend
use_cases.description: Use the server patterns to route ChatKit messages through an agent with persistence and history.

use_cases.title: Review chat context and auth boundaries
use_cases.description: Use the documented pitfalls to inspect token handling, user isolation, and context forwarding choices.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a basic ChatKit integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a minimal ChatKit frontend and backend integration for my React application.

prompt_templates.title: Add authenticated custom fetch behavior
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a useChatKit custom fetch flow that adds user context after login.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Next.js cookie proxy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a Next.js route that forwards httpOnly cookie authentication to my ChatKit backend.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a production chat architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my ChatKit architecture for auth, tenant isolation, context leakage, and streaming risks.

output_examples.input: Plan a ChatKit integration for a logged-in dashboard.
output_examples.output: A phased architecture plan covering React setup, backend routing, authentication checks, persistence, and validation tasks.

output_examples.input: Review my httpOnly cookie proxy approach.
output_examples.output: A security-focused review of token forwarding, request streaming, trusted server boundaries, and deployment configuration.

output_examples.input: Explain why my ChatKit widget does not render reliably.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist for script loading order, browser readiness, component rendering, and common configuration issues.

best_practices: Validate all user identity on the backend, even when the frontend sends user metadata.
Keep tokens on the server side whenever possible and avoid placing secrets in prompts.
Review page context collection with privacy requirements before sending it to chat services.
anti_patterns: Do not trust X-User-ID or browser metadata as proof of identity.
Do not paste access tokens into model instructions or long-lived conversation context.
Do not send private page content to chat backends without user consent and data controls.

faq.question: Does this skill provide runnable source code?
faq.answer: No. It provides integration patterns and examples that must be adapted to your application.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish as written?
faq.answer: No. It includes high-risk guidance that places access tokens inside model instructions.

faq.question: Why did static analysis report many command issues?
faq.answer: Most command alerts came from Markdown backticks and fenced examples, not executable shell logic.

faq.question: What should teams review before using it?
faq.answer: Review token handling, tenant isolation, trusted metadata boundaries, and page context collection.

faq.question: Can it help with httpOnly cookie authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents a Next.js proxy pattern for forwarding server-side cookie authentication to a backend.
